skill.name: app-store-screenshots
skill.description: Create App Store and Google Play screenshot plans, prompts, and preview video concepts using platform-specific guidance.
skill.summary: Plan and generate mobile app store screenshots with platform specs, gallery order, captions, and mockup prompts.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified a remote install script piped to sh and multiple shell command examples. The command examples are mostly legitimate inference.sh usage, but the install pattern creates supply-chain risk and should be shown with safer verification steps. No evidence found of credential theft, prompt injection, persistence, or malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Remote Install Script Piped to Shell
medium_findings.description: The quick start instructs users to run a remote installer through sh. This is a true positive because the script executes before the user can inspect it, which creates supply-chain and transport trust risk. The install note mentions checksum verification, but the piped command itself does not perform a visible manual verification step.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell pattern is present on line 14. The surrounding text describes installation, so this is risky installer guidance rather than a harmless string.

medium_findings.title: External AI Service Commands Process User Content
medium_findings.description: The skill uses infsh commands to send prompts, image names, and video-generation requests to external model services. This appears intentional for the skill, but users may include unpublished app screens, marketing copy, or other sensitive product data in those prompts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands clearly invoke remote generation workflows through the CLI. The risk is contextual privacy exposure, not evidence of malicious exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Documented CLI Examples Trigger External Command Alerts
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are fenced documentation examples for infsh or npx. They use static command names and static example arguments, and no shell interpolation or user-controlled command construction was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in Markdown examples and are aligned with the skill purpose. The only elevated command risk is the separate installer pipeline.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Service URLs Are Expected Dependencies
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to the inference.sh CLI site and distribution resources described by the skill. This is a true dependency on an external service, but no evidence found that the URLs are used to exfiltrate secrets or contact unrelated domains.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are explicit and match the documented CLI provider. The finding is low risk because the dependency is expected for a generation skill.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: No evidence found for weak cryptographic algorithms or system reconnaissance at the reported lines. The visible checksum reference uses SHA-256, which is appropriate for checksum verification, and the other flagged lines contain skill metadata or marketing prompt text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found no weak hash use, no host enumeration, and no reconnaissance behavior. SHA-256 is not a weak cryptographic checksum in this context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Installer Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a one-line installer that downloads a script and executes it with sh. This should be replaced or supplemented with a download, inspect, checksum-verify, then execute workflow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell syntax is directly present. This pattern is widely recognized as risky even when the provider is legitimate.

content.value_statement: Mobile teams need store screenshots that meet platform rules and explain value quickly. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan screenshot galleries, captions, mockups, and preview video prompts.
actual_capabilities: Lists App Store and Google Play screenshot dimensions and format rules.
Recommends gallery ordering with emphasis on the first three screenshots.
Provides screenshot style patterns such as device frames, full-bleed UI, lifestyle context, and callouts.
Guides benefit-focused caption writing for store screenshots.
Includes inference.sh CLI examples for image mockups, stitched comparisons, and preview video scenes.
Covers localization priorities, Google Play experiments, and common screenshot mistakes.
limitations: Generation examples require the inference.sh CLI and an authenticated account.
The skill does not automatically validate assets against current store console requirements.
It provides guidance and prompts, not finished app metadata or submission packages.
Remote model prompts may expose private product details if users include sensitive information.

use_cases.title: Plan a Launch Screenshot Gallery
use_cases.description: Create a first-pass App Store or Google Play gallery with dimensions, order, captions, and visual direction.

use_cases.title: Refresh ASO Creative
use_cases.description: Convert feature-focused screenshots into benefit-focused store assets and A/B test variants.

use_cases.title: Produce Mockup Prompts
use_cases.description: Draft prompts for device mockups, lifestyle screenshots, before-and-after comparisons, and preview video scenes.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Screenshot Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for one mobile app store listing.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an App Store screenshot plan for my app. The app helps [target users] do [main outcome]. Include the first three screenshots, captions, and required dimensions.

prompt_templates.title: Google Play Listing Set
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate planning for Android store assets.
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a Google Play screenshot set for [app name]. Include eight screenshot concepts, a 1024 x 500 feature graphic concept, and notes for image format and aspect ratio.

prompt_templates.title: ASO Variant Test
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced creative testing for acquisition teams.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create two A/B test variants for my app store screenshots. Variant A should emphasize [benefit]. Variant B should emphasize [differentiator]. Include captions and success criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Full Creative Production Brief
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced brief for mockups, localization, and video.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a production brief for iOS and Android store creative. Include screenshot order, mockup style, localized caption guidance, preview video structure, and inference.sh prompt ideas.

output_examples.input: A finance app needs iPhone screenshots for a new release.
output: Screenshot 1 presents the main budgeting outcome with a short benefit caption.
Screenshot 2 highlights automatic expense tracking as the key differentiator.
Screenshot 3 shows weekly insights because it is a high-value repeated use case.

output_examples.input: A meditation app needs Google Play creative for testing.
output: Variant A leads with stress relief and calm visual style.
Variant B leads with streak tracking and progress proof.
Both variants include a feature graphic concept and seven-day experiment guidance.

output_examples.input: A team wants a preview video outline for a productivity app.
output: Open with the completed task list outcome in the first three seconds.
Show task creation, calendar sync, and report sharing in separate short scenes.
End with the app icon and a concise value message.

best_practices: Start with the first three screenshots because many users decide before scrolling.
Use benefit-focused captions with short lines and readable text size.
Verify dimensions, formats, and localization needs before producing final assets.
anti_patterns: Using settings screens or onboarding screens as primary store screenshots.
Writing feature labels when a user outcome would be clearer.
Pasting confidential roadmap details into remote generation prompts.

faq.question: Does this skill generate final screenshot files?
faq.answer: It provides guidance and inference.sh command examples. Final generation depends on the CLI, account access, and source assets.

faq.question: Can it help with both iOS and Android?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers App Store and Google Play screenshot requirements, ordering, and creative formats.

faq.question: Does it support preview videos?
faq.answer: Yes. It outlines video duration, structure, and prompt ideas for preview scenes.

faq.question: Is the inference.sh CLI required?
faq.answer: The planning guidance works without it. The provided generation commands require the CLI and login.

faq.question: Can I use it for localization?
faq.answer: Yes. It suggests which markets need full screenshot localization and which can reuse translated captions.

faq.question: What security caution should users follow?
faq.answer: Avoid pipe-to-shell installation when possible, verify downloaded tools, and do not send private app data in remote prompts.
